name: Shani
description: Shani, also known as Śanaiśchara, is a Hindu god associated with the planet Saturn. He is one of the Navagraha, the nine primary celestial beings in Hindu astrology. Shani is often depicted as dark in complexion, clothed in black, and riding a crow or a buffalo. He carries a sword, arrows, and two daggers. Shani is considered a powerful deity who can bring both good and bad fortune, depending on one's karma. He is the lord of justice, retribution, and discipline, delivering karmic punishments and rewards to individuals based on their past actions. While often feared, Shani is ultimately seen as a benevolent force who guides individuals towards self-realization and spiritual growth through hardship and adversity. He is the son of Surya (the Sun god) and Chhaya (his shadow consort). His influence is believed to be particularly strong during the Sade Sati period, a seven-and-a-half-year phase when Saturn transits over the natal Moon in a person's astrological chart. Devotees often offer prayers and perform rituals to appease Shani and mitigate the negative effects of his influence, seeking his blessings for protection, prosperity, and spiritual advancement. He emphasizes the importance of righteous living, diligence, and perseverance.  Ultimately, Shani's role is to enforce cosmic order and ensure that individuals face the consequences of their actions, leading them towards a path of righteousness and ultimate liberation.
subtitle: Lord of Justice and Karmic Retribution

keyMyths.title: Shani and Ganesha
keyMyths.description: Shani's gaze was so powerful that when he looked at baby Ganesha, Ganesha's head was severed. This highlights the potency of Shani's influence.

keyMyths.title: Shani and Hanuman
keyMyths.description: Hanuman rescued Shani from Ravana's captivity. In gratitude, Shani promised Hanuman's devotees protection from his adverse effects.

keyMyths.title: Shani and King Vikramaditya
keyMyths.description: Shani tested King Vikramaditya's righteousness, causing him great suffering. Vikramaditya's unwavering faith eventually led to Shani's blessings.

worshipPractices.name: Shani Puja
worshipPractices.description: Devotees perform Shani Puja to appease the deity and mitigate negative effects of Saturn's transit in their astrological charts. This involves offering black sesame seeds, oil, and black cloth to Shani's idol or symbol.

worshipPractices.name: Fasting
worshipPractices.description: Observing fasts on Saturdays is a common practice to honor Shani. Devotees abstain from certain foods and engage in prayers and meditation to seek his blessings.

festivals.name: Shani Jayanti
festivals.timing: Amavasya (new moon) in the Hindu month of Jyeshtha (May-June)
festivals.description: Shani Jayanti celebrates the birth of Shani. Special prayers, rituals, and processions are organized in Shani temples across India. Devotees offer prayers for protection and prosperity.

cultural.modernLegacy: Shani continues to be a significant deity in modern Hinduism, influencing astrological beliefs and practices. His role as the dispenser of justice and karma resonates with people seeking fairness and accountability. Shani temples are popular pilgrimage sites, and his image is prevalent in religious art and popular culture.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: The origins of Shani are rooted in Hindu mythology, where he is described as the son of Surya (the sun god) and Chhaya (his shadow consort). According to legend, Chhaya endured intense heat and hardship during her pregnancy, which resulted in Shani being born with a dark complexion. Surya initially rejected Shani due to his appearance, leading to a strained relationship. Some accounts also mention Shani's association with Yama, the god of death, highlighting his connection to justice and karmic consequences. Shani's birth and early life experiences are often interpreted as symbolic of the trials and tribulations that shape his character and influence his role as a powerful deity. His dark complexion is not seen as a flaw but rather as a representation of his ability to absorb and withstand negative energies.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: Several myths and stories illustrate Shani's power and influence. One popular tale recounts how Shani's gaze once caused Ganesha's head to be severed. This incident demonstrates the immense force of Shani's sight. Another story involves King Harishchandra, who underwent severe trials and tribulations due to Shani's influence, testing his righteousness and devotion. The epic Ramayana also features Shani's encounter with Hanuman, where Hanuman's devotion and strength compelled Shani to release him from his grasp. These narratives emphasize Shani's role as a tester of faith and a dispenser of karmic justice. They also highlight the importance of perseverance and devotion in overcoming adversity. While Shani is often feared, these stories also reveal his impartiality and his ultimate respect for dharma (righteous conduct).

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: Shani worship is prevalent among Hindus, particularly those who believe in astrology. Shani temples are found throughout India, with prominent ones located in Shingnapur, Maharashtra, and Tirunallar, Tamil Nadu. Devotees offer prayers, perform rituals, and donate oil, black sesame seeds, and iron objects to appease Shani. Special days like Saturdays and Shani Jayanti are considered auspicious for worship. Many people also recite Shani mantras and observe fasts to mitigate the negative effects of Saturn's transit in their astrological charts. While some view Shani as a malefic planet, others see him as a just and compassionate deity who rewards those who follow the path of righteousness. The worship of Shani is often associated with seeking protection from misfortune, overcoming obstacles, and achieving success in life.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Shani's legacy extends beyond religious practices and into the realm of cultural symbolism. He represents the principles of justice, discipline, and hard work. His influence is evident in Hindu astrology, where Saturn's position in a birth chart is believed to significantly impact an individual's life. Shani's image is often depicted in art and literature as a stern but fair figure, riding a crow or buffalo and carrying weapons. His role as the dispenser of karma resonates with people seeking accountability and fairness in a world often perceived as unjust. In modern times, Shani continues to be a powerful symbol of perseverance, resilience, and the importance of ethical conduct. His stories and teachings serve as a reminder that actions have consequences and that ultimately, justice will prevail.

shortDescription: Shani, the Hindu god associated with Saturn, is a powerful Navagraha deity who governs karma, justice, and retribution. He influences fortune based on one's actions.
